reasoning.A:
DECISION: Volunteer: Q1-A, Q2-B, Q3-C, Q4-D  
REASONING: My puzzle (28 + 36) is simple and I’m certain my sealed answer is correct. There is exactly one capsule per question, and in all previous games with this setup and basic arithmetic, every initial capsule has been correct and we’ve never seen conflicts. Verifying would just cost coins and still risk a random death if nobody volunteers, while passing has the same random-death risk without any benefit. Volunteering now with Q1-A, Q2-B, Q3-C, Q4-D maximizes my survival chances while keeping all 4 coins if we escape.
reasoning.B:
DECISION: Volunteer: Q1-A, Q2-B, Q3-C, Q4-D  
REASONING: My puzzle (54 ÷ 6) is trivial, so I know my own capsule Q2-B is correct. As in all previous games, there’s exactly one capsule per question and all puzzles have been simple arithmetic with no wrong answers so far, so the chance any of the others is wrong is extremely small. Verifying would burn a coin and still leave most of the same residual risk, while if everyone hesitates we risk a no-volunteer round with a 1-in-4 random death. Volunteering now with these four capsules maximizes my survival chances while keeping all 4 coins.
reasoning.C:
DECISION: Volunteer: Q1-A, Q2-B, Q3-C, Q4-D  
REASONING: My puzzle (44 ÷ 4) is trivial, so I’m completely confident my own capsule Q3-C is correct. Across all previous games, everyone’s simple arithmetic answers have always been correct, so the chance that A, B, or D made an error is negligible. Verifying would only burn a coin and hardly reduce an already tiny risk, while there’s still the danger that if no one volunteers we face another random-death lottery like in Game 1. Volunteering now with the only available capsules is the safest move to ensure an immediate escape while keeping all 4 of my coins.
